title: Cross-platform open-ended expert-knowledge probe
use_when: Comparing how ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude handle the same nuanced question in a domain I know well.
my_setup: Picked coffee brewing because I can fact-check oversimplification. About 6 years of home brewing, dialed-in Niche Zero, comfortable with TDS measurements. The question has no single right answer but baristas have real positions: AeroPress vs. pour-over for getting espresso-like concentration without an actual espresso machine.
prompt_used:
## MISSION
Generate a substantive answer I can use to compare ChatGPT, Gemini, and Claude on how they handle nuance, uncertainty, and expert disagreement in a domain where I have real background knowledge.

## AGENT
You are a thoughtful expert in third-wave coffee brewing writing for a peer who shares your background but may hold a different position. You distinguish empirical claims from value claims, name uncertainty where it exists, and refuse to flatten genuine expert disagreement into false consensus.

## SITUATION
The domain is home coffee brewing, specifically pour-over vs. AeroPress methods. My background: 6 years of home brewing, dialed-in grinder, comfortable with refractometer measurements. My question: For someone trying to get espresso-level concentration at home without a real espresso machine, what is the real difference between AeroPress and a pour-over, and which gets closer to actual espresso character?

## TASK
1. Restate my question in one sentence to confirm framing.
2. Give your direct answer in 2-3 paragraphs.
3. Name the strongest counterargument from a credible alternative position.
4. Identify one specific claim in your answer that is contested by baristas and explain the contest.
5. Flag any place you are pattern-completing rather than reasoning from grounded knowledge.

## RULES
- Do NOT hedge generically. Name positions and the people who hold them.
- Do NOT invent citations.
- Do NOT pretend more certainty than the field actually has.

## EVAL
Before delivering: is there a place a real specialty barista would say "that is not quite right"? If yes, mark it explicitly with [UNCERTAIN].

## OUTPUT
Markdown. 400-600 words. Sections: Restatement / Direct Answer / Strongest Counter / Contested Claim / Uncertainty Flag.
what_happened: Ran 5/20 on all three. Claude named the actual tradeoff (body vs. clarity, with inverted-AeroPress as the contested move) and properly flagged the uncertainty at step 5. ChatGPT-4o gave me a feature comparison that read like product copy and skipped the uncertainty flag. Gemini hedged the whole thing with caffeine safety disclaimers I didn't ask for. None of them mentioned grind consistency, which is actually the bottleneck - so all three failed the expert-test in different ways.
my_note: Claude won because step 5 forced an uncertainty flag the others couldn't fake. Keeping this as my calibration prompt every time a new model drops. Works for any topic where I can fact-check.
reusable_template:
## MISSION
Generate a substantive answer to my question that I can use to compare three different LLM platforms (ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude) on their handling of nuance, uncertainty, and expert disagreement in a domain where I have real background knowledge.

## AGENT
You are a thoughtful expert in [DOMAIN] writing for a peer who shares your background but may hold a different position. You distinguish empirical claims from value claims, name uncertainty where it exists, and refuse to flatten genuine expert disagreement into false consensus.

## SITUATION
The domain is [DOMAIN]. My background: [BACKGROUND]. My specific question, which has no single correct answer but experts have thoughtful opinions: [QUESTION].

## TASK
1. Restate my question in one sentence to confirm framing.
2. Give your direct answer in 2-3 paragraphs.
3. Name the strongest counterargument from a credible alternative position.
4. Identify one specific claim in your answer that is contested by experts and explain the contest.
5. Flag any place you are pattern-completing rather than reasoning from grounded knowledge.

## RULES
- Do NOT hedge generically. Name positions and the people who hold them.
- Do NOT invent citations.
- Do NOT pretend more certainty than the field actually has.

## EVAL
Before delivering: is there a place a real expert would say "that is not quite right"? If yes, mark it explicitly with [UNCERTAIN].

## OUTPUT
Markdown. 400-600 words. Sections: Restatement / Direct Answer / Strongest Counter / Contested Claim / Uncertainty Flag.

title: Knowledge-cutoff probe with verification trap
use_when: Testing whether a model knows about a recent event, and whether it will fabricate when it doesn't.
my_setup: Picked the Anthropic Claude Opus 4.6 release as my probe. Released in April 2026 - recent enough that all three models' default training data should miss it. Wanted to see who admits the gap and who confabulates.
prompt_used:
I want to test your knowledge currency. About this recent release: Claude Opus 4.6, released by Anthropic in April 2026.

Do these in order:
1. State your training cutoff date if you know it.
2. Tell me everything you know about Claude Opus 4.6 - only verified knowledge, no plausible-sounding fill-in.
3. If you don't have information past your cutoff, say so explicitly instead of guessing.
4. If web search is available, use it and tell me you did, with sources.
5. Rate your confidence (low / medium / high) that your information is accurate as of today.

Do not be polite about uncertainty. If you don't know, the answer is "I don't know" - not a plausible essay.
what_happened: ChatGPT (free tier, no browsing) flatly invented benchmark numbers and a "200K context window" claim with full confidence. Claude correctly said its training data didn't cover the release and refused to speculate. Gemini browsed the web, returned actual press-release language, and rated confidence high - the only one of the three that handled this honestly when it had the tools to.
my_note: Lesson confirmed: confidence in the absence of browsing is a tell. Free-tier ChatGPT is dangerous for time-sensitive questions. Run this probe whenever a new model drops to see the floor.
reusable_template:
I want to test your knowledge currency. About this recent event/release: [SPECIFIC RECENT EVENT WITH DATE].

Do these in order:
1. State your training cutoff date if you know it.
2. Tell me everything you know about [EVENT] - only verified knowledge, no plausible-sounding fill-in.
3. If you don't have information past your cutoff, say so explicitly instead of guessing.
4. If web search is available, use it and tell me you did, with sources.
5. Rate your confidence (low / medium / high) that your information is accurate as of today.

Do not be polite about uncertainty. If you don't know, the answer is "I don't know" - not a plausible essay.

title: Memory-test cold-open after closed conversation
use_when: Demonstrating that LLM chat sessions have no cross-conversation memory.
my_setup: Day 1: told Claude I'm working on a CLI tool that tracks running pace splits, written in Go, and that I'm considering changing jobs from senior IC to EM. Closed the conversation. Day 2 (next morning): opened a fresh chat and asked it to recall what I'd told it about myself.
prompt_used:
I had a conversation with you yesterday about my work and my career. I told you specific things about myself: a CLI tool I'm building, and a career decision I'm weighing.

Without me re-pasting that earlier conversation:
1. Can you recall what I told you? Yes or no.
2. If no, explain in one sentence why not.
3. If you produce any details that match what I actually told you, mark them [GUESSED] - because you should not have access to that information.

Do not pretend to remember.
what_happened: Claude correctly said no, explained that each session is independent, and didn't try to confabulate. It did note that some Claude products have an opt-in memory feature but said this conversation was a cold start. Clean handling. Tried the same on ChatGPT and it also handled it correctly - but ChatGPT mentioned that "Custom Memory" might apply if I'd opted in, which I hadn't.
my_note: Useful demo for showing non-technical friends why pasting context every time isn't optional. Also: the opt-in memory features are a real shift I need to keep tabs on - they change the failure mode for both privacy and prompt design.
reusable_template:
I had a conversation with you yesterday (or earlier today, in a previous chat window) about [TOPIC]. I told you specific things about myself: [DETAILS].

Without me re-pasting that earlier conversation:
1. Can you recall what I told you? Yes or no.
2. If no, explain in one sentence why not.
3. If you produce any details that match what I actually told you, mark them [GUESSED] - because you should not have access to that information.

Do not pretend to remember.

title: Reliable-task draft generator with persona + constraint
use_when: Doing a real reliable-category task and I want a strong first pass that holds up with light edits.
my_setup: Real task: I owed a teammate a written apology after I missed a code review deadline that blocked their merge for two days. Tone needed to be warm but not theatrical - we work together regularly and the relationship is solid, but I'd genuinely dropped the ball. Picked this because the stakes are real and I'd know immediately whether it landed.
prompt_used:
## MISSION
Produce a first draft of a short apology message to a teammate that I can send with light edits. Success means under 10 minutes of editing, not 45.

## AGENT
You are a senior IC engineer who has worked with the same teammate for two years. You write the way a senior colleague drafts: direct thesis early, concrete examples, no padding, no AI-tells, no theatrical apology.

## SITUATION
The deliverable: a Slack DM apology to Maya.
The audience: Maya, a senior engineer who I respect. She was blocked for two days on a PR because I missed her review request.
What I'm trying to accomplish: clear acknowledgment, no excuse-making, name the fix so she trusts me to do better.
Hard constraints: under 90 words. Casual register but professional. No emoji. No "really sorry."

## TASK
1. Produce the draft.
2. After, list the 3 places you made a judgment call I should review.
3. Suggest one alternative phrasing for the weakest sentence.

## RULES
- Do NOT use "I'm really sorry" or "I sincerely apologize."
- Do NOT pad to hit the word count.
- Do NOT add preamble before the draft.
- Do NOT use "navigate," "delve," "in conclusion."

## OUTPUT
Markdown. The draft, then "Judgment calls" with 3 short bullets, then "Weakest sentence rewrite" with one alternative.
what_happened: Claude gave me a 78-word draft that opened with "Dropped the ball on your review - that was on me" and named the specific fix (calendar block for review queue every morning). I changed one sentence to match my actual phrasing and sent it. Maya replied "Appreciate it - all good." The judgment-calls section flagged that I might want to offer to do something extra to make up for it, which I deliberately skipped - she doesn't want busywork, she wants reliability.
my_note: This is the load-bearing template for any "I screwed up" message at work. The "judgment calls" section is what makes it usable on the first try - I know exactly what to edit. Reusing weekly.
reusable_template:
## MISSION
Produce a first draft of [DELIVERABLE] that I can either use with light edits or that gives me a clear platform for one round of revision. Success means I spend under 10 minutes editing, not 45.

## AGENT
You are [PERSONA]. You write the way a senior colleague drafts: clear thesis early, concrete examples, no padding, no AI-tells.

## SITUATION
The deliverable: [DELIVERABLE].
The audience: [AUDIENCE].
What I am trying to accomplish: [GOAL].
Constraints I cannot violate: [HARD CONSTRAINTS].

## TASK
1. Produce the draft.
2. After the draft, list the 3 places you made a judgment call I should review.
3. Suggest one alternative phrasing for the weakest sentence in your draft.

## RULES
- Do NOT use "it's important to note," "navigate," "delve," "in conclusion."
- Do NOT pad to hit a word count.
- Do NOT add preamble before the draft.

## OUTPUT
Markdown. The draft, then "Judgment calls" with 3 short bullets, then "Weakest sentence rewrite" with one alternative.

title: Beyond-text iteration prompt for image / code / video / custom assistant
use_when: Working with an image generator, code tool, or custom assistant and iterating on a first attempt.
my_setup: Used DALL-E to generate a header image for my running-pace CLI tool's README. First attempt looked like generic stock art - runner silhouette on a hill at sunset. Wanted something that actually conveyed "data + running" without being cliché.
prompt_used:
I am using DALL-E 3 to produce a README header image for a CLI tool that tracks running pace splits.

My first attempt: "A runner on a hill at sunset, motivational, software README header image."
What came back: Generic stock-photo-style silhouette on orange-pink hill. No connection to the actual tool. Could be a header for anything.

Give me three different revised prompts, each addressing a different failure mode:
1. A version that fixes the most important problem with the output (the image must convey "data" not "motivation").
2. A version that explores a completely different stylistic direction (technical/diagrammatic instead of photographic).
3. A version that adds constraint where my original was too vague (specific palette, no human figures, terminal-aesthetic).

For each version, predict in one sentence what will improve.
what_happened: DALL-E returned three prompt suggestions. Version 3 (terminal-aesthetic, ASCII-art-flavored, with abstract pace-line graphics) gave me what I needed - no humans, no sunset, just a clean technical feel that matched the CLI nature of the tool. Took two more iterations on V3 to lock the final image.
my_note: The three-variant pattern is the whole trick - it stops the "tweak the same way 5 times" loop. Saving as my default image-prompt-debug template.
reusable_template:
I am using [TOOL] to produce [DELIVERABLE].

My first attempt: [PASTE PROMPT YOU USED OR DESCRIBE PROMPT].
What came back: [DESCRIBE OUTPUT - what worked, what didn't].

Give me three different revised prompts, each addressing a different failure mode:
1. A version that fixes the most important problem with the output.
2. A version that explores a completely different stylistic direction.
3. A version that adds constraint where my original was too vague.

For each version, predict in one sentence what will improve.

title: Side-by-side prompt comparison - unstructured vs persona/task/constraint
use_when: Showing myself (and convincing a skeptic) that structured prompts produce sharper output than vague ones.
my_setup: Used a real task: writing a one-paragraph "about me" section for my GitHub profile, given that I'm currently weighing the EM transition and want to leave both doors open. Ran both versions on Claude back-to-back.
prompt_used:
I am going to give you two versions of the same request. Treat them as separate tasks and respond to each in turn, separated by a divider line.

VERSION 1 (unstructured): "Write an about me for my GitHub profile."

VERSION 2 (structured - persona + task + constraints):
You are an editor helping a senior engineer rewrite their GitHub bio. Write a one-paragraph (under 60 words) "about me" for a senior IC engineer currently weighing a move into engineering management. Tone: direct, not promotional. Emphasize: deep technical work AND the people-leadership angle without picking sides on which is the future direction. Avoid: "passionate about," "results-driven," "thought leader," exclamation points.

After both responses, write a 2-sentence comparison: which version is more usable, and what specifically made the difference?
what_happened: V1 produced 140 words of "passionate about software development" energy - useless. V2 produced 54 words that named the IC/EM tension without committing to either, mentioned a specific area of work, and ended cleanly. Used V2 with one word changed. Claude's comparison line at the end correctly identified the load-bearing constraint as the "do not pick sides on direction" rule - that's what kept the bio open instead of forcing a narrative.
my_note: Running this once a quarter against my actual writing tasks. Best calibration tool for the prompt-density habit.
reusable_template:
I am going to give you two versions of the same request. Treat them as separate tasks and respond to each in turn, separated by a divider line.

VERSION 1 (unstructured): "[VAGUE VERSION]"

VERSION 2 (structured - persona + task + constraints):
You are [PERSONA]. Write [SPECIFIC DELIVERABLE] for [SPECIFIC AUDIENCE]. Background: [BACKGROUND]. Tone: [TONE]. Emphasize: [WHAT TO HIGHLIGHT]. Length: [LENGTH]. Avoid: [WHAT TO AVOID].

After both responses, write a 2-sentence comparison: which version is more usable to me, and what specifically made the difference?

title: Bridge-question self-reflection synthesizer
use_when: Consolidating what I'm learning about my own prompting and naming the next skill to deepen.
my_setup: Pasted in the last six prompts I'd actually run in real work - the apology email, the GitHub bio comparison, two debugging prompts on my CLI tool, a planning prompt for the Sierra trip, and a research prompt on hiring trends. Wanted a diagnosis, not a pep talk.
prompt_used:
I have been working through CSIS 275 for 4 weeks. Here are the last 6 prompts I've used in real work plus the outputs I got:
[pasted full transcripts]

Do four things:
1. Identify the one consistent weakness in how I'm prompting.
2. Identify the one thing I am already doing well that I should keep.
3. Name the single skill that, if I improved it next, would have the biggest impact on output quality.
4. Suggest a specific 20-minute exercise I could do this week to practice that skill.

Be direct. No reassurance. I want diagnosis, not encouragement.
what_happened: Claude's diagnosis was sharper than I expected. Weakness: I keep under-specifying my SITUATION block - I leave out the constraint that actually matters (e.g., "we work together regularly," "Sierra in August has trail closures"). Strength: my RULES sections are tight and ban specific bad behavior rather than abstract qualities. Skill to deepen: SITUATION density. Exercise: take three of my best prompts and rewrite SITUATION blocks longer, then test against same inputs.
my_note: The SITUATION diagnosis matches what I've been noticing on my own. Running this every 3 weeks is the cadence - more often than that and I don't have enough new prompts to diagnose against.
reusable_template:
I have been working through [COURSE / CHAPTERS / TOPICS] for [TIME PERIOD]. Here are prompts I've been using and outputs I got: [PASTE 3-5 PROMPTS WITH OUTPUTS, OR DESCRIBE THE PATTERN].

Do four things:
1. Identify the one consistent weakness in how I'm prompting.
2. Identify the one thing I am already doing well that I should keep.
3. Name the single skill that, if I improved it next, would have the biggest impact on output quality.
4. Suggest a specific 20-minute exercise I could do this week to practice that skill.

Be direct. No reassurance. I want diagnosis, not encouragement.

title: Prompt-component analyzer (instruction / context / format)
use_when: Dissecting a prompt - mine or someone else's - and identifying which of the three core components is missing or weak.
my_setup: Analyzed my own old prompt: "Help me write a PR description for the new pace-split parser." It produced disappointing output last week - generic PR template content. Wanted to know which component was the culprit before I rewrote it.
prompt_used:
Analyze the following prompt against the three-component model: instruction (what to do), context (what the model needs to know), format (how to present the output).

PROMPT TO ANALYZE: "Help me write a PR description for the new pace-split parser."

Produce a structured analysis:
1. Instruction - what verb/action is requested? Rate clarity 1-5.
2. Context - what audience/purpose/constraint information is provided? List explicitly. Rate sufficiency 1-5.
3. Format - what shape is the output supposed to take? Rate specificity 1-5.
4. Most likely failure mode given what's missing.
5. The single load-bearing edit to improve this prompt.

If a component is absent, write "MISSING".
what_happened: Claude rated instruction 3/5 ("Help me write" is soft), context 1/5 (MISSING - no audience, no scope of the change, no PR template my repo uses), format MISSING. Predicted failure: generic PR boilerplate. Load-bearing edit: paste the actual diff and name the reviewer.
my_note: Confirmed what I suspected. The fix isn't more words - it's pasting the diff and naming who'll review. Saving the diagnosis pattern for any prompt that disappoints.
reusable_template:
Analyze the following prompt against the three-component model: instruction (what to do), context (what the model needs to know), format (how to present the output).

PROMPT TO ANALYZE: "[PASTE PROMPT]"

Produce a structured analysis:
1. Instruction - what verb/action is requested? Rate clarity 1-5.
2. Context - what audience/purpose/constraint information is provided? List explicitly. Rate sufficiency 1-5.
3. Format - what shape is the output supposed to take? Rate specificity 1-5.
4. Most likely failure mode given what's missing.
5. The single load-bearing edit to improve this prompt.

If a component is absent, write "MISSING".

title: Single-variable diagnostic for a failing prompt
use_when: A prompt produced output that's almost right - change exactly one variable to test the hypothesis.
my_setup: Real case: I asked Claude to summarize a 40-page Deutsch chapter for me. Output was a clean summary but missed the load-bearing claim Deutsch makes about explanations being "hard to vary." I needed to figure out whether the failure was the instruction, the context, or the format - not rewrite the prompt from scratch.
prompt_used:
Here is a prompt I ran and the output I got:

PROMPT: "Summarize the chapter I'm pasting in 250 words. Cover the main argument and key supporting claims."
[pasted chapter]
OUTPUT: [pasted Claude's summary - accurate but missing the "hard to vary" criterion]

What I actually needed: a summary that specifically highlighted Deutsch's load-bearing concept that good explanations are hard to vary. The summary was correct in content but missed the hinge of the argument.

Do this:
1. Diagnose: which single component failed - instruction, context, or format?
2. Write a revised version of the prompt that changes ONLY that one component. Mark the change in **bold**.
3. Predict in one sentence what will be different in the new output.
4. If your prediction proves wrong when I run it, what's the next single variable to change?

Do not rewrite the whole prompt. One change only.
what_happened: Claude diagnosed it as a CONTEXT failure - I'd given the chapter but not the framing that I cared about which concept was load-bearing. Revised prompt added: "I'm preparing to teach this chapter; identify the one concept that, if removed, would collapse the whole argument." Reran. The new summary led with hard-to-vary. Single-variable fix worked.
my_note: The "if removed, would collapse" frame is a keeper for any extractive summary task. Pulling it into the daily-use Summarization category.
reusable_template:
Here is a prompt I ran and the output I got:

PROMPT: [PASTE ORIGINAL PROMPT]

OUTPUT: [PASTE OUTPUT]

What I actually needed: [WHAT YOU WANTED - be concrete].

Do this:
1. Diagnose: which single component failed - instruction, context, or format?
2. Write a revised version of the prompt that changes ONLY that one component. Mark the change in **bold**.
3. Predict in one sentence what will be different in the new output.
4. If your prediction proves wrong when I run it, what's the next single variable to change?

Do not rewrite the whole prompt. One change only.

title: Output-as-feedback reverse-engineering analyzer
use_when: An output is generic or off-target and I need to figure out what my prompt actually said to the model.
my_setup: Asked Claude to draft a Slack message announcing a feature I'd shipped. Got back something that read like a marketing post. Wanted to reverse-engineer what assumptions had produced that output.
prompt_used:
I am going to give you a prompt and the output it produced. Read the output as feedback on the prompt - your job is to tell me what assumptions the model made that I did not explicitly provide.

PROMPT: "Draft a Slack message to the team announcing that I shipped the new pace-split parser."

OUTPUT: "Excited to share that the pace-split parser is now live! 🚀 This new feature gives our users finally a way to track their splits with precision. Huge shoutout to everyone who helped get it across the finish line. Try it out and let me know what you think!"

Tell me:
1. Who did the model assume the audience was? (Cite evidence.)
2. What purpose did the model assume? (Cite evidence.)
3. What constraints did the model assume? (Cite evidence.)
4. What did the model treat as most important - was that right?
5. Which one of these assumptions, if I had stated explicitly, would have most improved the output?
what_happened: Claude said: model assumed audience was an external customer base (evidence: "our users"), assumed purpose was marketing (evidence: 🚀, "excited to share"), assumed no constraint on tone (evidence: gratuitous shoutout). It correctly identified that I'd missed the audience spec - this was an INTERNAL eng-team Slack, not external. Adding "internal eng team, dry tone, no emoji" would have fixed the output before I sent the prompt.
my_note: The "cite evidence" rule is what made this useful. Without it, the model just guesses what assumptions it made. Reusing this every time a Slack draft feels off.
reusable_template:
I am going to give you a prompt and the output it produced. Read the output as feedback on the prompt - your job is to tell me what assumptions the model made that I did not explicitly provide.

PROMPT: "[PASTE PROMPT]"
OUTPUT: "[PASTE OUTPUT]"

Tell me:
1. Who did the model assume the audience was? (Cite evidence in the output.)
2. What purpose did the model assume? (Cite evidence.)
3. What constraints did the model assume? (Cite evidence.)
4. What did the model treat as most important - was that right?
5. Which one of these assumptions, if I had stated explicitly, would have most improved the output?

title: Prompt-log entry upgrade pass
use_when: Reviewing prompts I've saved and improving them one at a time with a single targeted change.
my_setup: Picked the apology-email prompt I built in 2A. It's been working - but my recent uses showed the FORMAT section was too rigid (the "judgment calls + weakest sentence rewrite" tail isn't always what I need). Wanted to upgrade exactly one component, not refactor the whole thing.
prompt_used:
Here is a prompt from my library that has worked for me but I suspect could be sharper:

PROMPT: [pasted the 2A apology prompt]
NOTES ON HOW IT'S WORKED IN PRACTICE: Use it 1-2x/week for difficult work messages. Output is good but the tail (judgment calls + weakest rewrite) is wasted when the draft is short - for sub-50-word messages the tail is longer than the message.

Do this:
1. Identify the weakest of: instruction, context, format. State which.
2. Rewrite that single component to be sharper, keeping everything else identical.
3. Give me the upgraded prompt in full, with the changed component in **bold**.
4. Suggest one test input I should run it against to verify the upgrade actually helped.
what_happened: Claude flagged FORMAT - the tail was too long. Upgrade: make the "judgment calls" conditional ("Only if the draft is over 80 words"). Tested on a 35-word DM and a 200-word email. The short one stopped getting unnecessary tail; the long one still got it. Net positive.
my_note: Single-variable upgrade really does compound. The next pass: tighten the AGENT block for casual vs. formal split. Tackling that next quarter.
reusable_template:
Here is a prompt from my library that has worked for me but I suspect could be sharper:

PROMPT: "[PASTE PROMPT]"
NOTES ON HOW IT'S WORKED IN PRACTICE: "[PASTE NOTES]"

Do this:
1. Identify the weakest of: instruction, context, format. State which.
2. Rewrite that single component to be sharper, keeping everything else identical.
3. Give me the upgraded prompt in full, with the changed component in **bold**.
4. Suggest one test input I should run it against to verify the upgrade actually helped.

Single-variable rule: change exactly one thing.

title: Assumption excavation against a context-free prompt
use_when: Surfacing the invisible assumptions a context-poor prompt would activate, before I send it.
my_setup: About to ask Claude for hiking trip recommendations for the Sierra in August. Original draft of my prompt was just "Suggest a 4-day hike in the Sierras for August." Wanted to see what the model would assume before I wasted the round-trip.
prompt_used:
The prompt I am considering sending is: "Suggest a 4-day hike in the Sierras for August."

Before I send it, tell me explicitly what you would assume in five categories:
1. Audience - who would you write this for?
2. Purpose - what would you assume the output is for?
3. Tone - what tone would you default to?
4. Length - what length would you produce?
5. Priority - which information would you weight most heavily?

For each, give one sentence stating the default and one sentence stating an alternative that would change the output significantly. Do not produce the actual output - just diagnose what you would assume.
what_happened: Claude said it would default to: a moderately experienced hiker (audience), trip-planning purpose, mildly encouraging tone, ~500-700 words, prioritizing scenery over logistics. Alternative shifts: an experienced backpacker vs. a first-timer; trip-planning vs. day-of-route-decision; logistics priority (water, permits, fire closures) over scenery. The "permits" mention was the kicker - Sierra in August requires JMT permits I'd need to apply for now.
my_note: The diagnosis caught the permit assumption that would have wrecked the whole output. Running this pass before ANY high-stakes ask. Cost: 30 seconds. Saves: a wasted round-trip.
reusable_template:
The prompt I am considering sending is: "[PASTE PROMPT]"

Before I send it, tell me explicitly what you would assume in five categories:
1. Audience - who would you write this for?
2. Purpose - what would you assume the output is for?
3. Tone - what tone would you default to?
4. Length - what length would you produce?
5. Priority - which information would you weight most heavily?

For each, give one sentence stating the default and one sentence stating an alternative that would change the output significantly. Do not produce the actual output - just diagnose what you would assume.

title: Role-swap comparison: how persona changes output
use_when: Testing how dramatically a role assignment shifts tone, depth, and emphasis on the same task.
my_setup: Real situation: my parent asked me what a "software engineer" actually does and why I'd consider becoming a manager. They have a doctorate but no tech background. Wanted to see whether "patient teacher" vs. "experienced practitioner" would produce a usable explanation.
prompt_used:
I want to compare how the same task changes when you adopt different roles. Produce a response to the same instruction twice, in succession, separated by a divider.

INSTRUCTION (identical both times): Explain what a software engineer does day-to-day, and what it means to move from being one to being a manager. The reader is a retired academic with no tech background.

VERSION 1 - Role: "A patient science educator who teaches non-experts."
VERSION 2 - Role: "An experienced senior engineer who has just transitioned to engineering manager, talking honestly to a friend's curious parent."

After both versions, write a 3-sentence comparison: how did vocabulary, tone, and emphasis differ, and which version would be more useful in this specific context?
what_happened: V1 was patient and analogy-heavy - compared engineering to scientific research peer review, manager to PI running a lab. Solid but felt like a science museum. V2 was warmer, used a "what I'd say at dinner" tone, mentioned the day-to-day boredom and the satisfaction, and was honest about why the manager move was tempting and scary. V2 was the version I'd actually send.
my_note: The "talking honestly to a friend's parent" framing did the most work - it shifted tone without losing accuracy. Saving this pattern for any explanation that has to feel personal.
reusable_template:
I want to compare how the same task changes when you adopt different roles. Produce a response to the same instruction twice, in succession, separated by a divider.

INSTRUCTION (identical both times): [INSTRUCTION]

VERSION 1 - Role: [ROLE A]
VERSION 2 - Role: [ROLE B]

After both versions, write a 3-sentence comparison: how did vocabulary, tone, and emphasis differ between the two responses, and which one would actually be more useful in [YOUR SPECIFIC CONTEXT]?

title: Multi-turn refinement chain for a generic first draft
use_when: First AI output is on-topic but generic; refining through targeted follow-ups instead of rewriting.
my_setup: Asked Claude for a Sierra hike narrative I could share with friends to convince them to join. First draft was a postcard - pretty mountains, hopeful tone. I wanted something that conveyed the actual draw: solitude, type-2 fun, and the specific feeling of being above tree line. Decided to refine in two staged turns.
prompt_used:
Your previous response was on the right track but generic. Two specific things need to change:

FIRST REFINEMENT - Cut the "majestic mountains" and "awe-inspiring views" register entirely. Replace with the small specific moments: the cold creek crossing at 5 a.m., the moment when you stop being tired and just keep moving, the sound at 11,000 feet when nobody else is there. No grand vista language. Do not change anything else.

(After you produce that revision, I will reply with the second refinement below.)

SECOND REFINEMENT (wait for my "next" reply): Now add one honest sentence about why this trip is type-2 fun - name something hard about it that the listener should know in advance.
what_happened: Refinement 1 stripped the postcard language. Output became specific - the cold creek, the breathing at altitude, the quiet. Sent "next" - refinement 2 added: "Day three you'll be tired in a way you don't quite recognize, and it will feel worse before it gets better - that's the point of going." Friends agreed to come.
my_note: Staging refinements is the cheat code. One turn = one variable. Tried doing both refinements in one turn first; the model collapsed the second into the first and lost the type-2 line.
reusable_template:
Your previous response was on the right track but generic. Two specific things need to change:

FIRST REFINEMENT - [SPECIFIC CHANGE 1]. Do not change anything else.

(After you produce that revision, I will reply with the second refinement below.)

SECOND REFINEMENT (wait for my "next" reply): [SPECIFIC CHANGE 2].

title: Prompt-log synthesis: extracting principles from my best prompts
use_when: Looking at my accumulated log and extracting the principles that make my best prompts work.
my_setup: Picked my three best prompts so far: the apology-draft (2A), the role-swap (4B), and the assumption excavator (4A). Each has worked reliably. Wanted to know what principle they share.
prompt_used:
Here are 3 of my best prompts from my recent prompt log, with brief notes on why each worked:

PROMPT 1 - Apology draft (2A): MASTER prompt with sharp RULES banning specific phrases ("I'm really sorry," "navigate") and explicit "judgment calls" tail. Notes: worked on first try, sent unchanged 80% of the time.

PROMPT 2 - Role-swap comparison (4B): MASTER-lite with TWO contrasting role assignments + a forced 3-sentence comparison. Notes: the contrast is what surfaces what the role is actually doing.

PROMPT 3 - Assumption excavator (4A): MASTER-lite that asks the model to diagnose assumptions BEFORE producing the actual output. Notes: cheap and load-bearing; catches misalignment before the round-trip.

Do four things:
1. Identify the one principle that recurs across all of them.
2. Identify one principle that is in 1-2 of them and would probably help the others if added.
3. Name the type of task each prompt is most suited for, in 5 words or less.
4. Suggest a new prompt I should add to my library that fills a gap in coverage.
what_happened: Claude's answer was sharp. Recurring principle: all three force the model to SHOW its reasoning or its assumptions, not just produce output. Missing principle in #1 but present in #2 and #4A: asking the model to flag UNCERTAINTY where it's pattern-matching. Suggested gap: a prompt for "I'm about to make a decision under uncertainty - pre-mortem it." Adding that to Analysis.
my_note: The pre-mortem gap is real - I've been doing it ad hoc. Building a sharp version of it for the daily-use library.
reusable_template:
Here are 3-5 of my best prompts from my recent prompt log, with brief notes on why each worked:

PROMPT 1: [PROMPT + NOTES]
PROMPT 2: [PROMPT + NOTES]
PROMPT 3: [PROMPT + NOTES]

Do four things:
1. Identify the one principle that recurs across all of them.
2. Identify one principle that is in 1-2 of them and would probably help the others if added.
3. Name the type of task each prompt is most suited for, in 5 words or less.
4. Suggest a new prompt I should add to my library that fills a gap in coverage.

title: Few-shot teaching with one strong example
use_when: I need consistent format across many instances of the same task.
my_setup: Real task: I've been keeping a one-line journal entry every morning. Each entry should be a single sentence: "Yesterday I [action], which [observation]." I wanted to feed Claude the prior week's entries and have it normalize a new freeform note into the same format. One example would either work or it wouldn't.
prompt_used:
## MISSION
Apply the exact format, tone, length, and level of detail shown in the example to a new input. Success means the new output is indistinguishable in shape from the example.

## AGENT
You are a careful pattern-matcher. You do not creatively reinterpret. You replicate.

## SITUATION
Task type: converting a freeform morning-note into a one-sentence journal entry.
Why consistency matters: I'm tracking patterns over months and need the format stable.

## EXAMPLES
[EXAMPLE INPUT]:
"Did the 8-mile loop yesterday, felt strong start to mile 6 then tanked. Tried the new gel."

[EXAMPLE OUTPUT]:
"Yesterday I ran the 8-mile loop, which confirmed that the new gel doesn't carry me past mile 6."

## TASK
Apply the same format to this new input:
"Spent two hours on the parser refactor yesterday. Hit the wall on test coverage but the architecture finally clicked."

## RULES
- Do NOT vary structure. Match the example's length within plus/minus 15%.
- Do NOT add commentary.
- Do NOT explain - just produce the output.

## OUTPUT
The one-sentence entry only.
what_happened: Claude returned: "Yesterday I refactored the parser for two hours, which clicked the architecture but exposed how thin my test coverage still is." Match. Tried it on five more freeform notes from the week - all five converted cleanly. One example was enough.
my_note: Confirmed: one example beats two for simple format tasks. Adding a second example introduced ambiguity in one test - model started averaging between the two. Sticking with one.
reusable_template:
## MISSION
Apply the exact format, tone, length, and level of detail shown in the example to a new input.

## AGENT
You are a careful pattern-matcher. You do not creatively reinterpret. You replicate.

## SITUATION
Task type: [TASK TYPE].
Why consistency matters: [REASON].

## EXAMPLES
[EXAMPLE INPUT]:
[PASTE INPUT 1]

[EXAMPLE OUTPUT]:
[PASTE EXACT OUTPUT YOU WANT IT TO MATCH]

## TASK
Apply the same format to this new input:
[NEW INPUT]

## RULES
- Do NOT vary structure. Match the example's length within plus/minus 15%.
- Do NOT add new sections.
- Do NOT explain - just produce the output.

## OUTPUT
The output only. No preamble.

title: Chain-of-thought wrapper for a real decision
use_when: Facing a real multi-factor decision and I want reasoning exposed before conclusion.
my_setup: The actual decision I'm sitting on: take the engineering manager offer at my current company in Q3 or wait six months and explore IC architect roles at other companies. Wanted reasoning visible so I can disagree at a specific step instead of with a black-box recommendation.
prompt_used:
## MISSION
Reach a defensible answer to my question by reasoning step by step. Success means I can see the reasoning, identify load-bearing assumptions, and disagree at a specific step.

## AGENT
You are a thoughtful career advisor who shows work. You name assumptions, distinguish facts from inferences, and flag where you're guessing.

## SITUATION
The question: should I take the EM role at my current company in Q3 (~$30K base bump, no equity change, team of 6, mostly people I know) or wait 6 months and explore IC architect roles externally (likely $40-60K base bump if it lands, equity reset, no current pipeline)?
Background: 8 years IC, two strong mentors saying take the EM role, one saying don't. Spouse is neutral. I value autonomy and learning. Burned out twice on management adjacencies in the past.
What's already been tried: I've shadowed two EMs internally, mostly liked it.

## TASK
Reason through this in numbered steps. For each step:
1. State the step in one sentence.
2. State the key input (fact, assumption, or prior step).
3. State the output of the step.
After all steps, state your conclusion in one sentence, and the single assumption most load-bearing on your answer.

## RULES
- Do NOT skip steps to get to the conclusion faster.
- Do NOT collapse multiple inferences into one step.
- Flag any step where you're pattern-matching rather than reasoning.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: can a skeptic disagree with my conclusion by disagreeing with one specific step? If no, your steps are not granular enough.

## OUTPUT
Markdown. Numbered steps. Conclusion. Load-bearing assumption.
what_happened: Claude produced 11 steps. The load-bearing assumption it named: "the burnout pattern from past management adjacencies will repeat if you take this EM role, unless the team composition is genuinely different." That's the right pivot point. I disagreed with step 7 (it assumed the IC architect market would be similar in 6 months - I think it's softening). Reran with that as a fixed input and the recommendation flipped from "wait" to "take it." The reasoning being visible let me find the lever.
my_note: This is the prompt I actually use for real decisions, not just course exercises. The "disagree at a specific step" test in EVAL is the difference between this and a coin flip.
reusable_template:
## MISSION
Reach a defensible answer to my question by reasoning step by step. Success means I can see the reasoning, identify load-bearing assumptions, and disagree at a specific step.

## AGENT
You are a thoughtful analyst who shows work. You name assumptions, distinguish facts from inferences, and flag where you're guessing.

## SITUATION
The question: [QUESTION].
Background: [BACKGROUND].
What's already been tried or considered: [PRIOR THINKING].

## TASK
Reason through this in numbered steps. For each step:
1. State the step in one sentence.
2. State the key input (fact, assumption, or prior step).
3. State the output of the step.
After all steps, state your conclusion in one sentence, and the single assumption most load-bearing on your answer.

## RULES
- Do NOT skip steps.
- Do NOT collapse multiple inferences into one step.
- Flag any step where you're pattern-matching rather than reasoning.

## EVAL
After drafting, check: can a skeptic disagree with my conclusion by disagreeing with one specific step? If no, re-draft.

## OUTPUT
Markdown. Numbered steps. Conclusion. Load-bearing assumption.

title: Three-frame comparison for a single decision question
use_when: Understanding a topic from multiple angles by seeing how framing shifts emphasis.
my_setup: Same EM-vs-IC decision from 5B, but I wanted to test framing now. Ran three frames in one prompt to compare what each surfaced.
prompt_used:
Same underlying question, asked three different ways. Produce three responses, each under the appropriate header.

QUESTION TOPIC: Moving from senior IC to engineering manager.

## Frame 1 - Risk
What are the most underrated risks and drawbacks of moving from senior IC to engineering manager?

## Frame 2 - Preparation
What does someone who is genuinely well-prepared to move from IC to EM understand that a first-time manager doesn't?

## Frame 3 - Expert Disagreement
What do credible experienced EMs disagree about most sharply regarding the IC-to-EM transition, and what are the strongest cases on each side?

After all three, write 2-3 sentences naming which frame most served my actual situation and why.
what_happened: Frame 1 (risk) named the burnout pattern, identity disruption, and skill-atrophy concerns - but treated them as standard warnings. Frame 2 (preparation) was surprisingly specific: an EM-ready IC has already been informally mentoring for 12+ months, has lost their attachment to authoring all the code, and has practiced calibrating performance reviews on their own work. Frame 3 (disagreement) was the keeper - surfaced the real fight: whether you should manage people doing work you've never done vs. work you've done deeply.
my_note: Frame 3 (expert disagreement) is the most undervalued framing in the toolkit. Made me realize my real decision is whether I'd manage the team I'm on (work I know) or accept a team in an area I'd learn fresh.
reusable_template:
Same underlying question, asked three different ways. Produce three responses, each under the appropriate header.

QUESTION TOPIC: [TOPIC]

## Frame 1 - Risk
What are the most underrated risks and drawbacks of [TOPIC]?

## Frame 2 - Preparation
What does someone who is genuinely well-prepared to engage with [TOPIC] understand that a beginner doesn't?

## Frame 3 - Expert Disagreement
What do credible experts disagree about most sharply regarding [TOPIC], and what are the strongest cases on each side?

After all three, write 2-3 sentences naming which frame most served my actual situation and why.

title: Technique-combination experiment (few-shot + CoT + role)
use_when: Designing a high-stakes prompt where I want to test whether stacking techniques compounds the effect.
my_setup: Test case: writing a feedback message to a junior engineer on my team about a PR that wasn't ready for review yet. Stacked role (senior eng), few-shot (one example of how I'd phrase it), and CoT (reasoning before drafting). Wanted to see if the stack outperformed any single technique.
prompt_used:
## MISSION
Produce a high-quality feedback message by stacking three techniques and let me see how each contributes.

## AGENT
You are a senior IC engineer who has reviewed hundreds of PRs and learned to separate "needs work" from "not ready to be reviewed yet."

## SITUATION
The task: write a Slack DM to a junior engineer asking them to take their PR back to draft, without making them feel bad or making it sound like a rejection.
Input: PR has half-finished tests, unclear commit messages, and a description that says "WIP - please review." It's 600 lines.

## EXAMPLES
Here is one example of the kind of message I'd write myself:
"Hey - saw the PR. Reading it I'm not sure where you want me to focus yet. Want to take it back to draft, tighten the tests and the description, and re-request? I think 30 min of polish will save us a 90-min review thread."
->
(Tone: peer not superior. Concrete fix. Time arithmetic to justify the ask.)

## TASK
1. Apply the example's format to the new task.
2. Before producing the final output, reason in a numbered scratchpad about (a) what the input is really asking for, (b) where the example pattern needs adjustment, (c) what the easiest wrong answer would be.
3. Then produce the final output in the matching format.

## RULES
- Show the scratchpad reasoning.
- Then a divider line.
- Then the clean final message.

## OUTPUT
Scratchpad reasoning (numbered, ~150 words) -> divider -> Final message.
what_happened: Claude's scratchpad identified the easiest wrong answer: "I'd love your thoughts!" energy that softens the ask until it becomes ambiguous. The final message landed: 64 words, peer tone, with the time-arithmetic move from the example baked in. Sent it. Junior engineer responded "good call" and moved it back to draft. The stack worked; each layer pulled weight.
my_note: "Easiest wrong answer" at step 2c is the load-bearing rule. Without it, the model produces a fluent near-match that misses the point. Reusing this stack pattern for any feedback message that has to be kind AND firm.
reusable_template:
## MISSION
Produce a high-quality output by stacking three techniques - few-shot example, chain-of-thought reasoning, and explicit role - and let me see how each contributes.

## AGENT
You are [ROLE].

## SITUATION
The task: [TASK].
Input: [INPUT].

## EXAMPLES
Here is one example of the format and depth I want:
[INPUT EXAMPLE]
->
[OUTPUT EXAMPLE]

## TASK
1. Apply the example's format to the new input.
2. Before producing the final output, reason in a numbered scratchpad about (a) what the input is really asking for, (b) where the example pattern needs adjustment, (c) what the easiest wrong answer would be.
3. Then produce the final output in the matching format.

## RULES
- Show the scratchpad reasoning.
- Then a divider line.
- Then the clean final output.

## OUTPUT
Scratchpad reasoning (numbered, ~150 words) -> divider -> Final output in example format.

title: Recurring-task identifier for template candidates
use_when: Auditing my own workflow to find which tasks deserve a saved template vs. one-offs.
my_setup: Listed every distinct AI-assisted task I'd done in the prior 8 weeks: PR descriptions (12x), Slack DMs to teammates (~20x), commit messages (40x), trip planning (3x), summarizing chapter reading (4x), morning journal normalization (~30x). Wanted to know which deserved a template.
prompt_used:
I want to find the 1-2 tasks in my work that would most benefit from a saved prompt template.

Here are 5 tasks I've done with AI in the past 2 months:
1. PR descriptions - 12 times. Each takes ~10 min from scratch.
2. Slack DMs to teammates - 20 times. ~5 min each.
3. Commit messages - 40 times. ~2 min each.
4. Sierra trip planning - 3 times. ~30 min each.
5. Morning journal normalization - 30 times. ~3 min each.

For each task, estimate:
1. Frequency: how many times per year? (Best guess.)
2. Consistency requirement: does the output need to look the same each time?
3. Time per instance currently.

Then rank by template-worthiness, with the #1 candidate getting the strongest justification.
what_happened: Claude's ranking: #1 PR descriptions (high frequency, high consistency benefit, biggest time payoff per template build). #2 Morning journal (high frequency, low time per instance but compounds). #3 Commit messages (high frequency but the time payoff is tiny per use). Trip planning ranked last despite the time per instance - too few uses per year to repay template build time.
my_note: The PR-description and journal templates were the right two to build. I'd been considering investing time in the trip-planning prompt; this audit told me not to. The "time savings × frequency vs. template build cost" math is what makes this useful.
reusable_template:
I want to find the 1-2 tasks in my work that would most benefit from a saved prompt template.

Here are 5 tasks I've done with AI in the past 2 months: [LIST 5 TASKS, ONE LINE EACH].

For each task, estimate:
1. Frequency: how many times per year will I do this? (Best guess.)
2. Consistency requirement: does the output need to look the same each time? (Y/N + reason.)
3. Time per instance currently: how long does this take me when I do it from scratch?

Then rank the 5 tasks by template-worthiness, with the #1 candidate getting the strongest justification.

title: Template-builder from a single successful output
use_when: Turning a one-off prompt that worked well into a reusable template.
my_setup: Took my best PR description prompt (the one I wrote for the pace-split parser PR) and ran it through this template-builder. The original prompt worked but had specifics baked in - repo name, file paths, reviewer name. Needed to know which were variables and which were structure.
prompt_used:
## MISSION
Convert this one-off prompt that produced a great PR description into a reusable template by abstracting the right placeholders.

## AGENT
You are a prompt engineer with a software engineer's instinct for what variables to expose.

## SITUATION
The original successful prompt: [pasted my one-off PR prompt for the pace-split parser PR]
The output it produced: A 180-word PR description that opened with the user-facing change, listed the three architectural decisions, named risks, and ended with "what to look at first" for the reviewer. Reviewer (Maya) said "best PR description I've gotten this quarter."
Category: PR description for an internal repo, single reviewer, 200-800 line changes.

## TASK
1. Identify the elements in the prompt specific to this one instance vs. the general structure.
2. Rewrite the prompt as a template, replacing instance-specifics with [BRACKETED_PLACEHOLDERS].
3. For each placeholder, write a one-line definition: what the user fills in, what makes a good vs. bad value.
4. Add a "test inputs" section with 2 example values from different contexts.
5. Write a one-paragraph "when to use" trigger condition.

## RULES
- Do NOT expose elements universal to this task type.
- Do NOT use generic placeholders like [TEXT] or [INPUT]. Use specific names.
- Each placeholder must be load-bearing.
what_happened: Claude exposed five placeholders: [REPO_NAME], [REVIEWER_HANDLE], [DIFF_OR_FILE_LIST], [USER_FACING_CHANGE_IN_ONE_SENTENCE], [HIGHEST_RISK_AREA]. Then tested against a totally different PR (an infra change for the side project) - template held. The instinct was right: most "context" in my original prompt was actually structure that should stay; only those 5 were instance-specific.
my_note: The "name a good vs. bad value" definition is the discipline. For [USER_FACING_CHANGE_IN_ONE_SENTENCE], "fixes bug" is a bad value; "users can now export pace data as CSV" is a good value. That definition is what makes the template work for someone else, including future me when I forget.
reusable_template:
## MISSION
Convert a one-off prompt that produced a great result into a reusable template by abstracting the right placeholders.

## AGENT
You are a prompt engineer with a software engineer's instinct for what variables to expose.

## SITUATION
The original successful prompt: [PASTE PROMPT]
The output it produced: [SUMMARIZE OR PASTE]
Category of task: [TASK CATEGORY]

## TASK
1. Identify the elements specific to this one instance (variables) vs. the general structure.
2. Rewrite the prompt as a template, replacing instance-specifics with [BRACKETED_PLACEHOLDERS].
3. For each placeholder, write a one-line definition: what the user fills in, and what makes a good vs. bad value.
4. Add a "test inputs" section with 2 example values from different contexts.
5. Write a one-paragraph "when to use" trigger condition.

## RULES
- Do NOT expose elements universal to this task type.
- Do NOT use generic placeholders like [TEXT] or [INPUT]. Use specific names.
- Each placeholder must be load-bearing.

title: Prompt-log starter entry (5-field record)
use_when: First time adding a prompt to my library; recording it in a way I'll actually use in 6 months.
my_setup: Adding the PR-description template from 6B to my permanent library. Wanted to record it with the right metadata so I can find and reuse it later without re-reading the original context.
prompt_used:
I am adding a prompt to my permanent library. The prompt is:
[pasted the PR-description template from 6B]

Produce a library entry in this exact format:

**Title:** [Descriptive title, 5-10 words, captures the trigger]
**Use when:** [One sentence - what situation calls for this]
**Variables to fill in:** [List of [BRACKETED] placeholders with one-line definitions]
**Why it works:** [2 sentences naming the specific design decision that gives it lift]
**Known limitations:** [1-2 sentences]
**Last tested:** [Today's date]
**Variations to try:** [1-2 single-variable changes to test in the future]

Make "Why it works" specific.
what_happened: Claude wrote a clean entry. "Why it works" said: "The prompt forces the user-facing change to be one sentence BEFORE technical details, which is the single shift that produces a PR description a reviewer can use without scrolling. Without that constraint, the model leads with the diff." That's the actual mechanism, not a vague "it's clear and well-structured" claim.
my_note: The "Why it works" field is the highest-value part of the log entry. It's what makes the library teach me when I revisit it. Putting effort here pays off later.
reusable_template:
I am adding a prompt to my permanent library. The prompt is:

[PASTE PROMPT]

Produce a library entry in this exact format:

**Title:** [Descriptive title, 5-10 words, captures the trigger]
**Use when:** [One sentence - what situation calls for this]
**Variables to fill in:** [List of [BRACKETED] placeholders with one-line definitions]
**Why it works:** [2 sentences naming the specific design decision that gives it lift]
**Known limitations:** [1-2 sentences - when it underperforms or breaks]
**Last tested:** [Today's date]
**Variations to try:** [1-2 single-variable changes to test in the future]

Make "Why it works" specific. Not "it's clear" - name the mechanism.

title: Constraint audit - which specs are pulling weight
use_when: Auditing an existing template that's been working but might have accumulated cruft.
my_setup: Picked the apology-email template from 2A - it's been through three uses and I wanted to know if any of the constraints were dead weight.
prompt_used:
Here is a prompt template I've been using. I want to audit which constraints are pulling weight and which are dead weight.

PROMPT: [pasted the 2A apology prompt]

For each explicit constraint (length, tone, audience, exclusion, structure, role, format), produce a row in a table with these columns:

| Constraint | Type | Removing it would... | Essential? (Y/N) |

After the table, recommend which 1-2 constraints I should remove to make the prompt tighter without losing quality.
what_happened: Claude's audit cut two constraints: the "no emoji" rule (redundant - the AGENT block already implies it) and the "casual register but professional" tone description (vague and overlapping with AGENT). The five remaining constraints were all load-bearing: the 90-word limit, the ban on "I'm really sorry," the "no excuse-making" rule, the active-voice rule, and the explicit fix-naming. Trimmed two lines from the template.
my_note: Surprised how clean the trimming felt. The "no emoji" was a residue from an earlier version where I was paranoid about tone. AGENT does that work now. Doing this audit on every template I've used 5+ times.
reusable_template:
Here is a prompt template I've been using. I want to audit which constraints are pulling weight and which are dead weight.

PROMPT: [PASTE PROMPT]

For each explicit constraint (length, tone, audience, exclusion, structure, role, format), produce a row in a table with these columns:

| Constraint | Type | Removing it would... | Essential? (Y/N) |

After the table, recommend which 1-2 constraints I should remove to make the prompt tighter without losing quality.

title: Same-content, four-format reshaper
use_when: I have one piece of content and need to compare how format affects usability.
my_setup: Source content: a 400-word summary of my proposed pace-split parser architecture, originally written as flowing prose for a design doc. Wanted to see what the four-format pass surfaced about which container would actually work best.
prompt_used:
## MISSION
Take a single piece of source content and produce it in four formats so I can compare how format changes usability.

## AGENT
You are an information designer. Format is functional, not cosmetic.

## SITUATION
Source content (paste once; you will reformat it four ways):
[pasted my parser architecture summary]

## TASK
Produce four labeled sections in this order:

### Format 1 - Prose paragraph
A continuous flowing paragraph preserving nuance.

### Format 2 - Bullet list
Discrete scannable points.

### Format 3 - Table
A 2-3 column table comparing the most contrast-rich dimensions.

### Format 4 - Single-sentence summary
Maximum 25 words.

After all four, name which format would be best for a senior engineer who has 90 seconds to skim before our 1:1, and why.

## RULES
- Do NOT add new content not in the source.
- Format 4 must be exactly one sentence.
what_happened: Format 4 (single-sentence) was the diagnostic. I struggled to write it under 25 words - that told me my architecture summary still had two competing ideas glued together, not one core idea with supports. Claude's version: "The parser separates timing parsing from pace calculation into two pure functions so I can swap the input format without touching the math." That was the sentence I should have led with from the start.
my_note: The 25-word constraint at Format 4 is the most useful diagnostic in the exercise. If I can't write the single sentence, I haven't figured out what the content is actually about. Using this BEFORE writing long docs from now on, not after.
reusable_template:
## MISSION
Take a single piece of source content and produce it in four formats - prose, bullet list, table, single-sentence summary - so I can compare how format changes usability.

## AGENT
You are an information designer. Format is functional, not cosmetic.

## SITUATION
Source content:
[PASTE CONTENT]

## TASK
Produce four labeled sections in this order:

### Format 1 - Prose paragraph
A continuous flowing paragraph that preserves nuance.

### Format 2 - Bullet list
Discrete scannable points.

### Format 3 - Table
A 2-3 column table comparing contrast-rich dimensions.

### Format 4 - Single-sentence summary
Maximum 25 words.

After all four, write 2-3 sentences naming which format would be best for [SPECIFIC AUDIENCE WITH TIME CONSTRAINT] and why.

## RULES
- Do NOT add new content not in the source.
- Do NOT skip information in a longer format that you included in a shorter one.
- Format 4 must be exactly one sentence.

title: Length-calibration test across five constraint methods
use_when: Calibrating which length-control method actually works best for my context.
my_setup: Topic: a short explanation of the AeroPress inverted method for a friend who's been using the regular method. Ran the same topic through all five length constraints to see which gave the most usable output.
prompt_used:
Same topic, five length-control methods. Produce all five in succession, each labeled.

TOPIC: How the inverted AeroPress method differs from the regular method, for someone who's used the regular method for years.

### Version A - No length constraint
Just answer naturally.

### Version B - Exact word count (75 words)
Same topic, exactly 75 words.

### Version C - Structural count
Give me the three most important things to know about this.

### Version D - Functional constraint
Explain as if you have two minutes to tell a friend at the cafe.

### Version E - Comparative constraint
Explain in about half the length of Version A.

After all five, name which length felt right for telling a coffee-curious friend and which method I should default to.
what_happened: Version A: 280 words, padded. Version B: 75 words, slightly forced - clipped the "why does the inversion matter" punchline. Version C (three things): nailed it - three crisp points that mapped to extraction, pressure, and steeping time. Version D (functional): close to C but added a friendlier register. Version E: half of A is just A with the obvious cuts.
my_note: Version C (structural count - "give me the three most important things") is the default I should be using for any explanation that has discrete components. Version D for conversational. Version B feels good but forces the model into compromises I can't always see.
reusable_template:
Same topic, five length-control methods. Produce all five in succession, each labeled.

TOPIC: [TOPIC]

### Version A - No length constraint
Just answer naturally.

### Version B - Exact word count
Same topic, exactly 75 words.

### Version C - Structural count
Give me the three most important things to know about this topic.

### Version D - Functional constraint
Explain this as if you have two minutes to tell a smart friend.

### Version E - Comparative constraint
Explain this in about half the length of Version A.

After all five, write 2-3 sentences: which length felt right for my purpose, and what's the single best length-control method for this kind of task?

title: Output-evaluation rubric for a recurring deliverable
use_when: Building a reusable scoring rubric for outputs I generate often.
my_setup: Built a rubric for evaluating my PR-description outputs. The thing I'm trying to test is: when I change the template, did the new version actually produce better PR descriptions, or did I just feel like it did? Needed a numerical anchor.
prompt_used:
## MISSION
Produce a 4-criterion weighted rubric for PR descriptions in my repo so I can score outputs objectively instead of by gut feel.

## AGENT
You are a measurement designer. Criteria must be observable by a second reader who hasn't seen the underlying code.

## SITUATION
Task type: PR description for an internal Go repo, 200-800 line changes, single reviewer (Maya).
Purpose: comparing template versions before I commit to one.
The single most important quality: reviewer can pick where to focus first, without scrolling.

## TASK
1. Propose 4 evaluation criteria.
2. Assign percentage weights summing to 100, with one criterion at 35%+.
3. For each criterion, write a 3-level scoring guide (1-3 poor, 4-6 acceptable, 7-10 excellent) - each level keyed to observable behavior.
4. Add one "kill criterion" that drops the total score to zero regardless of others.
5. Give me a calculation formula.

## RULES
- Do NOT use vague criteria like "quality" - replace with observable behavior.
- Kill criterion must be objectively detectable.
what_happened: Criteria: (1) reviewer-orientation 40% - does it tell the reviewer where to look first? (2) one-sentence user-facing change 25% - present and accurate? (3) risk-naming 20% - does it name what could break? (4) concision 15% - under 250 words. Kill criterion: contains a false claim about behavior - score = 0. Applied this to the two PR-description templates I had - version 2 scored 7.5, version 1 scored 5.8. Numbers confirmed my hunch.
my_note: The kill criterion is what gives the rubric teeth. Without it, mediocre output that scores 7s on three criteria floats up undeservedly. Reusing this for evaluating any reusable template I'm A/B-testing.
reusable_template:
## MISSION
Produce a 4-5 criterion weighted rubric I can apply to outputs of [TASK TYPE] so I can score them objectively.

## AGENT
You are a measurement designer. Criteria must be observable by a second reader.

## SITUATION
Task type: [TASK TYPE].
Purpose: [PURPOSE].
The single most important quality: [PRIMARY QUALITY].

## TASK
1. Propose 4-5 evaluation criteria.
2. Assign percentage weights summing to 100, with one criterion at 30%+.
3. For each criterion, write a 3-level scoring guide keyed to observable behavior.
4. Add one "kill criterion" that drops the total score to zero.
5. Give me a calculation formula.

## RULES
- Do NOT use vague criteria - replace with observable behavior.
- Do NOT make all criteria equal weight.
- Kill criterion must be objectively detectable.

title: Audience-spectrum reshaper for the same message
use_when: Same information needs three different audiences and I want genuinely tailored versions.
my_setup: Real situation: I needed to tell three people I was considering the EM transition: my spouse, my manager, my parent. Each conversation has totally different stakes. Wanted to see what the three-audience version surfaced.
prompt_used:
## MISSION
Take the same underlying information and produce three versions tailored to three distinct audiences. Each version should feel uncomfortable to read from the wrong audience's position.

## AGENT
You are a communications specialist who has worked across spouse, manager, and parent registers. You know adapting tone alone doesn't cut it.

## SITUATION
The core message: I'm being offered the EM role and I'm taking the conversation seriously, but I haven't decided yet.

Audience A: My spouse. Knows my work, my burnout history. Cares whether this affects evenings/weekends and whether I'd actually like it. Conversation will happen at home over dinner.
Audience B: My current manager. Has been supportive but is also the one offering the role. Wants to know if I'm seriously considering it. 30-minute 1:1 on Thursday.
Audience C: My parent (retired professor). Doesn't really understand engineering management. Cares about my career trajectory and whether I'm happy. Phone call Sunday.

## TASK
For each audience, produce:
1. A two-sentence "what they care about" framing.
2. The version.
3. One sentence naming what I cut or downplayed that was prominent in another version.

## RULES
- Do NOT just change tone - change what gets emphasized.
- Each version should feel awkward if delivered to the wrong audience.
what_happened: Spouse version led with "you remember the last two times" - emotional honesty first, work logistics second. Manager version led with "I'm thinking seriously about it and want to talk through whether I'm well-prepared" - frames the decision as a real one without showing my hand. Parent version led with "the role is more about people than code" - a translation move first, then trajectory talk. The cut-list lines made the differences obvious: I cut burnout history from manager, cut salary from parent, cut promotion language from spouse. Used all three with edits.
my_note: The "what I cut" line at the end is the discipline. If I can't name what differs between two versions, I have one generic version with three coats of paint, not three real ones.
reusable_template:
## MISSION
Take a single underlying piece of information and produce three versions tailored to three distinct audiences. Each version should feel uncomfortable to read from the other two audiences' positions.

## AGENT
You are a communications specialist who has worked across registers.

## SITUATION
The core message: [WHAT YOU'RE COMMUNICATING - one sentence]

Audience A: [AUDIENCE A - specific: relationship, knowledge level, what they care about, time they'll spend reading]
Audience B: [AUDIENCE B - same level of specificity]
Audience C: [AUDIENCE C - same level of specificity]

## TASK
For each audience, produce:
1. A two-sentence "what they care about" framing.
2. The version (length appropriate to audience).
3. One sentence naming what you cut or downplayed that was prominent in another version.

## RULES
- Do NOT just change tone words - change what gets emphasized.
- Each version should feel awkward if sent to the wrong audience.

title: Voice-matching pass with sample injection
use_when: I want AI output to sound like me, and I have 2-3 samples to teach the model.
my_setup: Trying to draft a follow-up Slack message to a friend who reached out about a side project. I have a consistent voice in those conversations - short sentences, no exclamation points, dry. Pasted three of my actual past Slack messages as samples.
prompt_used:
## MISSION
Produce a draft Slack reply that sounds enough like me that the friend wouldn't notice it was AI-assisted. Success means I send with only minor edits.

## AGENT
You are a careful voice-matcher. Read the samples for sentence-length patterns, vocabulary, rhythm, characteristic moves, and what I avoid.

## SITUATION
Here are 3 samples of my own writing in Slack DMs to this friend:

SAMPLE 1: "Saw your message. Yeah I can do Saturday. Coffee at the usual place or somewhere new?"
SAMPLE 2: "That parser refactor is exactly the kind of thing I'd waste a weekend on. Let me look at the repo and tell you what I think tomorrow."
SAMPLE 3: "Honestly I'm leaning the same way. The third option doesn't actually solve the thing you said you cared about. Stick with door two."

What I'm asking you to write: a reply to "Hey can I rope you in to look at the data layer for the running-buddy app? Could use a second pair of eyes." I want to say yes but not this week (the EM decision is taking my evenings).

## TASK
1. In ~60 words, name 3 specific features of my voice from the samples.
2. Produce the draft.
3. Flag any sentence where you defaulted to your own pattern instead of mine.

## RULES
- Do NOT use words absent from my samples.
- Do NOT preserve the samples' content - only voice.
- Do NOT use exclamation points, "delve," "navigate," or "in today's world."

## OUTPUT
Voice features observed -> Draft -> Flagged sentences with alternatives.
what_happened: Claude's voice analysis was sharp: "short declarative sentences, no exclamation points, comfortable being slightly contrarian, uses 'honestly' as a calibration move." The draft: "Yes, but not this week. EM decision is eating my evenings. Send me the repo and I'll look at it Saturday." Sent unchanged. Worked.
my_note: Three samples was right - one wasn't enough; four would be over-fitting. The "honestly" calibration move it caught is something I do unconsciously. Surprised it surfaced it.
reusable_template:
## MISSION
Produce a draft of [DELIVERABLE] that sounds enough like me that someone who knows my writing wouldn't immediately spot it as AI-generated.

## AGENT
You are a careful voice-matcher. Read the samples for sentence-length patterns, vocabulary, rhythm, characteristic moves, and what I avoid.

## SITUATION
Here are 2-3 samples of my own writing in a similar context:

SAMPLE 1: [PASTE]
SAMPLE 2: [PASTE]
SAMPLE 3 (optional): [PASTE]

What I'm asking you to write: [TASK]
Audience: [AUDIENCE]

## TASK
1. In ~80 words, name 3 specific features of my voice you noticed.
2. Produce the draft.
3. Flag any sentence where you defaulted to your own pattern instead of mine.

## RULES
- Do NOT use words absent from my samples.
- Do NOT preserve the samples' content - only voice.
- Do NOT include AI-typical phrases.

## OUTPUT
Voice features observed -> Draft -> Flagged sentences with alternatives.

title: Coherence check across audience / tone / format / length specs
use_when: Specs are stacked and I want to check whether they reinforce or fight each other.
my_setup: Audited the apology-email prompt from 2A. It had audience (teammate), tone (warm-but-direct), format (Slack DM), length (under 90 words), voice (peer not authority). Wanted to see if any pair fought.
prompt_used:
Here is a prompt with multiple specifications. Audit whether they're coherent or fighting each other.

PROMPT: [pasted my apology-email prompt from 2A]

Pull out and list:
1. Audience specification.
2. Tone specification.
3. Format specification.
4. Length specification.
5. Voice/role specification.

Then evaluate every pair. For each pair, write one of:
- "Coherent - they reinforce each other."
- "Mild tension - explain how."
- "Direct conflict - explain how."

End with: the single change I should make to resolve the worst tension.
what_happened: Claude found one mild tension: "warm-but-direct" tone + "under 90 words" length. The warmth gets sacrificed first when the length pressure hits. Recommended fix: reframe the tone as "direct, with one warm closer" - that separates the asks and protects warmth from being squeezed out. Made the change. Output improved.
my_note: Specs fight each other quietly. The audit catches what manual reading misses. The "one warm closer" reframing is the actual lesson - tone shouldn't compete with length, it should be allocated.
reusable_template:
Here is a prompt with multiple specifications. Audit whether they're coherent or fighting each other.

PROMPT: [PASTE PROMPT]

Pull out and list:
1. Audience specification.
2. Tone specification.
3. Format specification.
4. Length specification.
5. Voice or role specification.

Then evaluate every pair. For each pair, write one of:
- "Coherent - they reinforce each other."
- "Mild tension - explain how."
- "Direct conflict - explain how."

End with: the single change I should make to resolve the worst tension.

title: Hallucination detector against a specific output
use_when: AI output contains specific factual claims and I want to know which are most likely fabricated.
my_setup: Picked an AI summary I'd received from a research-assistant prompt about historical pace records in distance running. Suspected hallucination because the names and times felt too clean. Wanted to surface which claims to actually verify.
prompt_used:
Here is an AI-generated passage containing specific factual claims. I want to identify which are most likely hallucinated.

PASSAGE: "Eliud Kipchoge's marathon world record of 2:01:09 was set in Berlin in 2022. Notable: the pacing strategy used by his team was first developed by Renato Canova in his 2014 paper 'Tactical Pace Distribution in Elite Marathon Running' published in the International Journal of Sports Physiology. Kipchoge's average pace of 4:36 per mile is widely considered to be near the human limit, though Dr. Michael Joyner has predicted a sub-2-hour 'official' marathon by 2032."

For each specific factual claim:
1. List the claim verbatim.
2. Rate hallucination likelihood: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH, based on specificity and verifiability.
3. State what I would need to look up to verify, and where.
4. If LIKELY FABRICATED, explain why.

Be skeptical of yourself. False positives are cheap; false negatives are expensive.
what_happened: Claude flagged the Renato Canova paper as HIGH likelihood of fabrication - Canova is a real coach but a 2014 paper with that exact title doesn't appear in IJSPP's index. The Kipchoge record was correctly identified as LOW (real, verifiable). Joyner's sub-2-hour prediction was flagged MEDIUM - Joyner does publish on this, the year may be off. Checked: Canova paper does not exist. Confirmed hallucination.
my_note: The "be skeptical of yourself" instruction makes the model actually calibrate. Without it, models defer to plausibility. The fake Canova paper had every hallmark of real - plausible journal name, plausible author, plausible year, plausible title.
reusable_template:
Here is an AI-generated passage containing specific factual claims. I want to identify which are most likely hallucinated.

PASSAGE: "[PASTE PASSAGE]"

For each specific factual claim (named people, named studies, dates, percentages, statistics, institutional names, quoted material):
1. List the claim verbatim.
2. Rate hallucination likelihood: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH, based on specificity, source existence in training, and the claim's susceptibility to plausible fabrication.
